metadata.title: Why Claude can write the right spreadsheet formula and the file still shows the wrong number
metadata.topic: Formula string vs. cached value in .xlsx
metadata.purpose: An .xlsx cell stores TWO things — the formula string and a cached last-computed value. A library that writes the formula sets only the string; the cache stays stale. Nothing evaluates it until a calculation engine (LibreOffice) opens the file and rewrites the cache.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro am_onyx) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.prerequisites: spreadsheet formulas, files store both formula and value
metadata.exclusions: OOXML XML internals, LibreOffice setup, circular references, formula-error types
metadata.visual_object: One cell split into two stacked layers — a formula layer (`=B5*(1+B6)`) and a cached-value layer showing a stale 100 that flips to 105 only when a "recalc" gear passes over it.
metadata.hook: An agent writes a correct formula into a cell, saves the file, reopens it — and the cell still shows the old answer, with no error.

beats.narration_text: Cell C5 is set to `=B5*(1+B6)` with B5=100, B6=0.05. Open the workbook: C5 reads 100, not 105. The formula is right; the number is stale — until a real engine recalculates.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: The formula is provably correct, yet the file reports the wrong value with no error — why can't the tool that wrote the formula also produce its answer?
An agent writes a correct formula into a cell, saves the file, reopens it — and the cell still shows the old answer, with no error.

An .xlsx cell stores TWO things — the formula string and a cached last-computed value. A library that writes the formula sets only the string; the cache stays stale. Nothing evaluates it until a calculation engine (LibreOffice) opens the file and rewrites the cache.
beats.visual: One cell split into two stacked layers — a formula layer (`=B5*(1+B6)`) and a cached-value layer showing a stale 100 that flips to 105 only when a "recalc" gear passes over it.
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: Write `=SUM(B2:B4)` over B2=10, B3=20, B4=30 into C1; save → C1's cache still reads 0. Run recalc → engine evaluates → cache rewritten to 60. Same file, same formula, correct number only after recalculation.

An .xlsx cell stores TWO things — the formula string and a cached last-computed value. A library that writes the formula sets only the string; the cache stays stale. Nothing evaluates it until a calculation engine (LibreOffice) opens the file and rewrites the cache.
